name: Jiangshi
body: Stiff with rigor and dressed in the robes of a Qing official, the Jiangshi cannot bend its limbs, so it moves in rigid hops, arms outstretched, hunting the warm breath and life-essence of the living. It is a corpse that failed to rest — improperly buried, dead far from home, or stirred by foul energy.

It is blind and tracks you by your breathing alone. The old advice is the strangest and the simplest: stand still, and hold your breath until it passes. A Taoist priest seals it with a strip of yellow paper inscribed in cinnabar, pressed to its brow, and it freezes mid-hop.
note: Folk wards include mirrors (which terrify it), sticky rice, the wood of a peach tree, and a threshold board high enough that stiff legs cannot clear it. The legend may grow from the real trade of "transporting a corpse home" over long roads in old China.
